turnings.name: Candlemas
turnings.explainer: The midpoint between winter solstice and spring equinox. Traditionally a day of candle-blessings and weather divination — "if Candlemas be fair and bright, winter will have another flight." The older Welsh and Gaelic tradition called this same turning Imbolc, the feast of St. Brigid, marking the first stirrings of spring beneath still-frozen ground.

entries.name: St. Brigid's Day / Imbolc
entries.desc: Welsh and Gaelic patron of hearth, healing, and early spring; the older name for the Candlemas turning.

entries.name: Candlemas
entries.desc: Midpoint of winter; candles blessed, weather divined.

entries.name: Mardi Gras / Shrove Tuesday
entries.desc: The last day before Lent; Mobile hosts the oldest Mardi Gras celebration in the United States.

entries.name: Ash Wednesday
entries.desc: The start of Lent in the Western Christian tradition.

entries.name: First peepers calling
entries.desc: Spring peeper frogs begin their evening chorus; Alabama's earliest nighttime sign of spring.

entries.name: St. David's Day / Dydd Gŵyl Dewi
entries.desc: Patron saint of Wales; leeks and daffodils traditionally worn.

entries.name: Welsh daffodils in bloom
entries.desc: The flower of Wales, and a Southern early-spring marker.

turnings.name: Quickening
turnings.explainer: An old English word for the first undeniable signs of life returning — when seeds swell past dormancy, sap rises, and the earth "quickens." The same root as "the quick and the dead." Lady Day on March 25, the Feast of the Annunciation, was the legal New Year in Britain until 1752.

entries.name: St. Joseph's Day / San Giuseppe
entries.desc: Major Sicilian-American feast; St. Joseph's altars and zeppole.

entries.name: Spring Equinox
entries.desc: Day and night of equal length; the astronomical start of spring.

entries.name: Lady Day / Feast of the Annunciation
entries.desc: The historic British New Year until 1752, and one of the four quarter days of the old legal calendar.

entries.name: Easter
entries.desc: Christian feast of the Resurrection; includes Palm Sunday, Good Friday, and Easter Sunday.

entries.name: Dogwoods bloom
entries.desc: Southern folk signal that it's safe to plant.

entries.name: St. George's Day
entries.desc: Patron saint of England and Catalonia; dragons and spring flowers.

entries.name: Arbor Day
entries.desc: Last Friday in April.

turnings.name: Beltane
turnings.explainer: The Welsh name for this turning is Calan Mai, the beginning of summer in the old Celtic reckoning. Bonfires were historically lit at dusk on May Eve. The modern May Day falls on the same date with different resonance — spring flowers, labor, May baskets.

entries.name: Beltane / Calan Mai
entries.desc: The Celtic and Welsh beginning of summer; bonfires historically lit at dusk on May Eve.

entries.name: May Day
entries.desc: Spring flowers, May baskets, and the international labor holiday.

entries.name: Mother's Day
entries.desc: Second Sunday in May.

entries.name: Memorial Day
entries.desc: Last Monday in May.

entries.name: Decoration Day
entries.desc: The older Southern tradition of cleaning family graves, predating and originally distinct from federal Memorial Day; observed by many Alabama families on the Sunday nearest Memorial Day.

entries.name: Fireflies emerge
entries.desc: Alabama's great early-summer spectacle.

entries.name: Mulberries ripen
entries.desc: Backyard forage across the South.

entries.name: Juneteenth
entries.desc: Commemorates the emancipation of enslaved people in Texas in 1865.

turnings.name: Midsummer
turnings.explainer: The summer solstice and the days around it. Shakespeare's Midsummer Night is June 23, the eve of St. John's Day — a date when bonfires were lit across both Wales and Sicily. The longest days of the year, when the sun seems to pause before turning back.

entries.name: Summer Solstice
entries.desc: The longest day of the year.

entries.name: St. John's Day / Midsummer proper
entries.desc: Bonfires observed across Wales and Sicily; Shakespeare's Midsummer Night is the eve before.

entries.name: Dog Days begin
entries.desc: The traditional Dog Days run July 3 through August 11, when Sirius the Dog Star rises with the sun.

entries.name: Feast of St. Rosalia
entries.desc: Patron of Palermo; major festa with processions and lights, observed wherever Sicilian-Americans settled.

entries.name: Figs ripen
entries.desc: Southern and Sicilian staple; sacred to Mediterranean tradition.

entries.name: Perseid meteor shower begins building
entries.desc: Peaks in mid-August around the Feast of St. Lawrence.

turnings.name: Lammas
turnings.explainer: From the Anglo-Saxon hlaf-mas, "loaf-mass" — the day when the first wheat of the harvest was baked into bread and brought to church to be blessed. The older Gaelic name is Lughnasadh. This is the turning of the year when summer's long labor starts paying out.

entries.name: Lammas / Loaf-Mass
entries.desc: The first wheat of the harvest blessed into bread; one of the four cross-quarter days of the old calendar.

entries.name: Feast of St. Lawrence / San Lorenzo
entries.desc: The Perseids peak on his feast; the shooting stars are traditionally called "the tears of St. Lawrence."

entries.name: Ferragosto / Feast of the Assumption
entries.desc: The great Italian summer holiday, rooted in both Roman and Catholic observance.

entries.name: Katydids begin
entries.desc: Southern folk tradition holds that first frost comes six weeks after the first katydid call.

entries.name: Labor Day
entries.desc: First Monday in September.

entries.name: Muscadines ripen
entries.desc: Native Southern grape, found wild along creek edges.

entries.name: Harvest Moon
entries.desc: The full moon nearest the autumn equinox; the brightest and earliest-rising full moon of the year.

turnings.name: Michaelmas
turnings.explainer: The Feast of St. Michael, September 29. One of the four historic quarter days of the British legal calendar — the day when rents came due, servants were hired, and the year turned toward its darker half. Michaelmas daisies bloom in Alabama right around now.

entries.name: Autumn Equinox
entries.desc: Day and night of equal length; the astronomical start of fall.

entries.name: Michaelmas / Feast of St. Michael
entries.desc: One of the four quarter days of the old British legal calendar, when rents came due and the year turned toward its darker half.

entries.name: Feast of St. Francis
entries.desc: Traditional day for the blessing of animals.

entries.name: Michaelmas daisies bloom
entries.desc: Purple asters, named for this turning.

entries.name: Hunter's Moon
entries.desc: The first full moon after the Harvest Moon.

entries.name: First frost window opens
entries.desc: Cardiff's historical first frost typically falls in early November.

entries.name: Persimmons ripen after first frost
entries.desc: Southern folk holds that frost sweetens them.

entries.name: Halloween / All Hallows' Eve
entries.desc: The eve of Hallowtide and its popular face.

turnings.name: Hallowtide
turnings.explainer: The old English name for the three-day arc of All Hallows' Eve, All Saints, and All Souls. The Welsh called this same turning Calan Gaeaf, winter's beginning. The Sicilian tradition of Il Giorno dei Morti — gifts left for children from deceased relatives — lives in this stretch of days.

entries.name: All Saints' Day / Calan Gaeaf
entries.desc: The Welsh beginning of winter and the first day of Hallowtide.

entries.name: All Souls' Day / Il Giorno dei Morti
entries.desc: In the Sicilian tradition, the day the dead return bearing gifts for children.

entries.name: Leonid meteor shower peaks
entries.desc: One of the year's reliable meteor showers.

entries.name: Thanksgiving
entries.desc: Fourth Thursday in November.

entries.name: St. Nicholas Day
entries.desc: European Christian tradition of shoes left out for small gifts.

entries.name: Earliest sunsets of the year
entries.desc: The sun sets earliest a week before the solstice itself.

entries.name: Santa Lucia
entries.desc: Observed especially in eastern Sicily; traditionally no wheat or bread is eaten, and cuccìa (a wheat berry pudding) is made.

turnings.name: Yuletide
turnings.explainer: The old Germanic name for the turning around the winter solstice, kept in English long after Christmas absorbed most of its practices. Traditionally a full season rather than a single day, running from solstice through Twelfth Night on January 5. The longest nights of the year, and the slow turn back toward light.

entries.name: Winter Solstice / Yule
entries.desc: The longest night of the year and the start of Yuletide.

entries.name: Christmas Eve / La Vigilia
entries.desc: In Sicilian tradition, the Feast of the Seven Fishes.

entries.name: Twelfth Night
entries.desc: The end of the twelve days of Christmas; traditionally the night to take down the greenery.

entries.name: Epiphany / La Befana
entries.desc: In Italian tradition, an old witch flies from house to house leaving gifts for children, older than Santa Claus in the Italian imagination.

entries.name: Martin Luther King Jr. Day
entries.desc: Third Monday in January.

entries.name: Dydd Santes Dwynwen
entries.desc: The Welsh Valentine's Day; patron saint of lovers.

entries.name: Groundhog Day
entries.desc: Descended directly from the Candlemas tradition of weather divination.
